title: Overcoming the World by Faith
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: Overcoming the World by Faith - Pastor Tito Lyro - 1 John 2_15-17.txt

outline.point: I. What Is 'the World' in 1 John 2:15-17?
subpoints: A. John uses world in multiple ways—a survey of the book
B. Here: the realm of sinful humanity in rebellion against God
C. Worldliness = human nature without God; love of the Father vs. love of the world

outline.point: II. What Overcoming the World Is NOT
subpoints: A. Not isolating yourself (sin travels with you to Everest)
B. Not winning power battles in culture or through politics
C. Not withdrawing like monks or Amish

outline.point: III. Four Essential Aspects of Overcoming the World
subpoints: A. Decide to overcome—Joshua's commitment; no neutrality exists
B. Own the freedom and perseverance you have in Christ (Romans 7)
C. Rise above worldly circumstances through Christ-centered contentment
D. Live a life of self-denial taught by grace (Titus 2:11-12)

outline.point: IV. The Practice of Overcoming: Four Prerequisites
subpoints: A. You must be born again (1 John 5:4)
B. You must have faith in Jesus Christ as Son of God
C. Fight the lust of the flesh, lust of the eyes, and pride of life
D. These are not legalism but the fruit of God's grace

outline.point: V. Summary: Four Ways We Overcome
subpoints: A. By believing in Jesus Christ as Son of God
B. By purifying our hearts through Christ-centeredness
C. By living according to what pleases God
D. By living for the unseen, eternal world

summary:
Expounding 1 John 2:15-17, Pastor Tito Lyro addresses the tension every Christian lives in: the already-not-yet space between what Christ has accomplished and his return to make all things new. He carefully defines what the 'world' means in this passage—not the physical creation or lost humanity as a whole, but the kingdom of darkness whose ruler and inhabitants are in rebellion against God—and shows why love for this world is incompatible with love for the Father.

The sermon distinguishes between false ways of overcoming worldliness (isolation, cultural power, withdrawal) and the genuine biblical approach: deciding to fight, owning your identity in Christ, practicing contentment, and living a life of self-denial produced by grace. Practical and honest throughout, Pastor Lyro even names television as his personal idol, modeling the transparency he calls for in the congregation. The sermon closes with four summary ways to overcome: believing in Christ as Son of God, purifying the heart, living to please God, and living for the eternal world to come.
transcript:
## The Already and the Not Yet

The Bible often describes the Christian life as a battle, a struggle, a difficult race. The Christian life is portrayed in terms of the tension between the already and the not yet: what Christ has already accomplished in the redemption of his elect, and the not yet of his return to make all things new. That is where we live.

Because that is where we live, there is great tension in our lives. John expresses this in 1 John. In chapter 5, verses 4–5, he speaks in terms of having overcome the world — something that happened in the past when we came to faith. But in today’s passage he speaks of overcoming the world as something still happening in our lives. John teaches us that love for the world is incompatible with love for the Father. As we overcome the world, we demonstrate that we love the Father, not the world.

This is consistent with what Jesus teaches in the Sermon on the Mount:

> *“No one can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or else he will be loyal to the one and despise the other.”* (Matthew 6:24)

One love must rule our life — a holy passion for God and the things of God.

## What Is “the World” in This Passage?

John uses the word *world* twenty-three times in 1 John, and he uses it differently each time. A survey of the book shows at least four uses: the physical universe; the system of this present age opposed to God; all people living at a particular point in history; and all ethnicities who have ever lived.

In our passage, John does not refer to the physical world. He uses the term to refer to a kingdom whose ruler and inhabitants are lost in sin and completely at odds with anything pleasing to God. Paul refers to the same group as “the kingdom of darkness.” World here is a realm in opposition to Christ and his church.

> *“Even if our gospel is veiled, it is veiled to those who are perishing, whose minds the god of this age has blinded, who do not believe, lest the light of the gospel of the glory of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine on them.”* (2 Corinthians 4:3–4)

So worldliness is human nature without God. In Christ we are a new creature:

> *“If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation; old things have passed away; behold, all things have become new.”* (2 Corinthians 5:17)

But vestiges of the old nature remain in us, and sanctification is about getting rid of them. Overcoming the world is part of that process — putting off what belongs to the old self and putting on what belongs to who we are in Christ (Ephesians 4:20–24).

## What Overcoming the World Is Not

**It does not mean isolating yourself.** If you go live on top of Everest alone, the world is still there with you — because you are there.

**It does not mean winning political or cultural power battles.** John does not mean overcoming the world by having a particular political party in power.

**It does not mean withdrawing like monks or the Amish.** A Christian is called to fight worldliness while in the world, even though he is not of the world.

**It does not mean Christianizing everything.** What God calls us to do is to disciple the nations, not to rearrange the furniture on the deck of the Titanic.

## Four Essential Aspects of Overcoming the World

**First: Make a decision to overcome.** It does not happen by accident. Joshua exemplifies this at the end of his life:

> *“Choose for yourselves this day whom you will serve… but as for me and my house, we will serve the LORD.”* (Joshua 24:15)

There is no neutrality — you are either serving God or serving Satan. Lukewarmness is the worst of all decisions. Moses is another example: by faith he chose to suffer with the people of God rather than enjoy the passing pleasures of sin, “esteeming the reproach of Christ greater riches than the treasures in Egypt” (Hebrews 11:24–26).

**Second: Own the freedom and perseverance you have in Christ.** Satan knows our weaknesses. But we must own the fact that sin no longer has dominion over us and that Satan is a defeated foe. Paul owned his identity in Christ even in the midst of struggling with sin:

> *“O wretched man that I am! Who will deliver me from this body of death? I thank God — through Jesus Christ our Lord! There is therefore now no condemnation to those who are in Christ Jesus.”* (Romans 7:24–8:1)

**Third: Rise above worldly circumstances through Christ-centered contentment.** Lack of contentment will keep us from overcoming the world.

> *“I have learned, in whatever state I am, to be content… I can do all things through Christ who strengthens me.”* (Philippians 4:11–13)

**Fourth: Live a life of self-denial taught by grace.** It is the grace of God that produces self-denial in us:

> *“For the grace of God that brings salvation has appeared to all men, teaching us that, denying ungodliness and worldly lusts, we should live soberly, righteously, and godly in the present age.”* (Titus 2:11–12)

Find the thing that is in the way and kill it. As John Owen said: kill sin — because if you do not, sin will kill you.

## The Practice of Overcoming: Two Prerequisites

**You must be born again.** You cannot overcome the world if you are not born again:

> *“For whatever is born of God overcomes the world.”* (1 John 5:4)

Regeneration takes us out of the sphere where Satan rules and into the family of God. The fascination of the world loses its appeal. Objectively, the world was overcome at the death and resurrection of Jesus Christ. Subjectively, we overcome the world at our new birth.

**You must have faith in Jesus Christ.** “This is what has overcome the world — your faith” (1 John 5:4). The content of what we believe and the faith we have in Jesus Christ — that is what overcomes.

## Three Battlefronts: 1 John 2:16

John names three ongoing arenas of conflict:

> *“For all that is in the world — the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life — is not of the Father but is of the world.”* (1 John 2:16)

**The lust of the flesh** — the desires left over from our old sinful and fallen nature. It would be wonderful if those desires all disappeared at the new birth, but they do not.

**The lust of the eyes** — the temptations that come from outside, everything in this world trying to pull us away from Christ.

**The pride of life** — the love of material possessions, the love of money, the boasting about what we have and who we are.

Fighting these three things means putting restrictions on ourselves — being careful about associations, entertainment, and how we talk and dress. This has nothing to do with legalism. Legalism is an attempt to earn righteousness before God by keeping rules. Working out our sanctification as a result of the grace of God poured into our hearts is not legalism.

## Summary: Four Ways We Overcome

We overcome the world by **believing in the Lord Jesus Christ as the Son of God** — there is no other way.

We overcome the world by **purifying our hearts through Christ-centeredness**.

We overcome the world by **living according to what pleases God**.

We overcome the world by **living for the unseen, eternal world that awaits us**:

> *“And the world is passing away, and the lust of it; but he who does the will of God abides forever.”* (1 John 2:17)

To live for this present world is to live for kindling — it will all be burned up at the coming of Jesus Christ. What God has given us is eternal and of far greater value. If we treasure Christ, the world will be a strange place to us as we sojourn toward the Promised Land.
